items.title: Customer onboarding lifecycle
items.description: Customer onboarding is the end-to-end process that moves a prospect to an active, serviceable relationship. It typically chains lead capture, identity and document collection, due-diligence checks, risk rating, internal approval, and account or product provisioning. Each stage produces evidence that is retained for audit, and a relationship is normally not activated until prior stages have passed their required checks. Onboarding software should model the lifecycle as explicit states with recorded transitions rather than free-form status flags so that no stage can be skipped silently.
items.applicability: Applies when building or validating onboarding workflows in banking or management-company middleware, where a new customer, member, or counterparty must be vetted before activation.
items.example: A new corporate client moves through states Captured to DocsCollected to DueDiligenceComplete to Approved to Activated, with a timestamped actor recorded at each transition.
items.limitation: Conceptual summary only; the exact stages, mandatory checks, and acceptance criteria are jurisdiction-specific and institution-specific and must be confirmed against current rulebooks and approved internal procedures. Not legal or compliance advice.

items.title: Account opening process
items.description: Account opening provisions a specific product or facility once a customer relationship is approved. It usually validates eligibility, captures product parameters such as currency, tariff, and limits, links the account to verified customer records, and assigns ownership and servicing responsibilities. Controls typically prevent opening an account before mandatory customer-level checks are complete and enforce that account attributes are internally consistent. Systems should treat account opening as a controlled creation event with its own approval and audit footprint rather than a simple data insert.
items.applicability: Applies when modelling product/account provisioning for banking, leasing, or fund-management middleware where accounts must be tied to a vetted customer.
items.example: An approved client requests a multi-currency settlement account; the system checks product eligibility, sets the currency and tariff, links the verified customer, and records an account-opened event with the approving officer.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; eligibility rules, required documents, and product constraints are scheme- and jurisdiction-specific and must be confirmed against current institutional policy. Not legal or compliance advice.

items.title: Mandate approval
items.description: A mandate defines who is authorised to act on an account or relationship and within what limits, for example which signatories may approve payments and up to what amount. Mandate approval validates the authority chain, records authorised parties and their thresholds, and binds them to the account before any instruction can be honoured. Mandates are usually versioned so that historical authority can be reconstructed for any past instruction. Software should enforce mandate checks at instruction time rather than relying on operators to remember current authority.
items.applicability: Applies to payment, transfer, and instruction handling in banking and management-company systems where account authority and signing limits must be enforced.
items.example: An account mandate authorises two named directors to jointly approve outgoing payments above a set threshold; an instruction breaching that rule is blocked and routed for exception handling.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; signing rules, thresholds, and authority structures vary by institution and jurisdiction and must be confirmed against approved mandate policy. Not legal advice.

items.title: Exception queues
items.description: Exception queues hold items that cannot complete straight-through processing and need human review, such as failed validations, breached limits, or mismatched references. A well-designed queue captures the reason for the exception, the originating transaction, an assignee, and an SLA clock so items are not lost. Items leave the queue only through an explicit resolution action that is recorded for audit. Treating exceptions as first-class, queryable work items keeps operational risk visible rather than buried in ad-hoc emails.
items.applicability: Applies when building operations consoles for payments, reconciliation, onboarding, or approvals where some items inevitably fall out of the automated path.
items.example: A payment that breaches a mandate limit lands in an exception queue with reason 'limit-exceeded', is assigned to a supervisor, and is either approved with override or rejected, with the decision logged.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; queue categories, SLAs, and escalation rules are institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved operating procedures. Not advice.

items.title: Reconciliation operations
items.description: Reconciliation compares two or more independent records of the same activity, such as an internal ledger versus a bank statement or a custodian feed, to confirm they agree. The process matches items by keys and tolerances, classifies unmatched items as breaks, and tracks each break to resolution. Reconciliation is normally run on a defined cycle and produces evidence that balances were proven for the period. Software should preserve the matching basis and the audit trail so that a past reconciliation can be re-explained.
items.applicability: Applies to settlement, nostro/vostro, ledger, and fund-accounting reconciliation in banking and management-company middleware.
items.example: A daily run matches internal ledger entries against a custodian feed; 1,200 of 1,210 items match within tolerance, and the remaining 10 are raised as breaks for investigation.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; matching keys, tolerances, and frequency depend on the instruments and institution and must be confirmed against approved reconciliation policy. Not advice.

items.title: SLA and KPI for operations
items.description: Operational performance is commonly tracked through service-level agreements (SLAs) that set time targets for activities and key performance indicators (KPIs) that measure throughput, quality, and timeliness. Typical measures include time-to-onboard, exception ageing, reconciliation break rate, and first-pass yield. Capturing these as measured fields, rather than estimates, lets operations managers spot bottlenecks and breaches early. Dashboards should distinguish leading indicators, which warn of future trouble, from lagging indicators, which confirm past performance.
items.applicability: Applies when instrumenting operations dashboards and reports for banking and management-company back-office processes.
items.example: An onboarding SLA targets activation within five business days; a KPI shows the rolling proportion of cases meeting it, flagging when the trend falls below target.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; specific SLA targets and KPI definitions are institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved service definitions. Not advice.

items.title: Escalation matrix
items.description: An escalation matrix maps the severity and age of an issue to the role or function that must act, and within what time. It ensures that stalled or high-impact items rise to progressively more senior owners rather than waiting indefinitely. A practical matrix encodes triggers (such as SLA breach, value thresholds, or risk flags), the next responsible level, and the notification path. Encoding escalation as data lets systems route automatically and prove, after the fact, that the right people were engaged at the right time.
items.applicability: Applies to exception handling, incident management, and approval bottlenecks across banking and management-company operations.
items.example: A reconciliation break unresolved after two days and above a value threshold auto-escalates from analyst to team lead, then to the operations manager if still open.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; severity bands, timings, and roles are institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved escalation policy. Not advice.

items.title: Operating-manager dashboard concept
items.description: An operating-manager dashboard consolidates the health of back-office processes into a single, role-appropriate view: queue depths, ageing exceptions, SLA breaches, reconciliation status, and pending approvals. The design goal is decision support, so it surfaces what needs attention now and lets the manager drill into the underlying items. Values should be read from pre-aggregated or cached snapshots so the view stays responsive and never blocks on slow downstream systems. The dashboard is a window onto authoritative records, not a separate source of truth.
items.applicability: Applies when building management consoles for banking or management-company operations that summarise multiple operational domains.
items.example: A single screen shows ten open exceptions, two SLA breaches, last night's reconciliation as balanced, and five payments awaiting a second approver, each clickable to its detail.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; the exact tiles and thresholds depend on the institution's processes and must be confirmed against approved operating procedures. Not advice.
